title: Rabbit ear mite treatment
subtitle: How to identify ear mites in rabbits and manage the initial response.
excerpt: Ear mites cause intense irritation and rapid crust build-up in the ear canal. Caught early, the condition responds well to treatment. The crusts should never be manually removed; doing so tears the skin beneath and dramatically worsens the infection.
sourceNotes: RSPCA, rabbit health guidance. RWAF (Rabbit Welfare Association and Fund), ear mite factsheet. BSAVA Small Animal Formulary, rabbit parasitology notes.

glossaryTerms.definition: The mite species responsible for ear canker in rabbits. Lives on the surface of the skin inside the ear canal, feeding on skin debris. Causes intense irritation, leading the rabbit to scratch and shake its head. The mite deposits crusty brown debris in the ear as it feeds and reproduces. Highly contagious between rabbits by direct contact.

content.text: ) are one of the most common external parasites in rabbits kept in groups or outdoors. The first sign is usually head shaking or scratching at the ears. Looking inside the ear, you will see brown, crusty debris, sometimes filling a large proportion of the canal.

In the early stage, there is a small amount of brown, honey-coloured crust at the base of the ear. In a more advanced case, the crust builds up into a thick, layered mass that can extend well into the ear canal. The rabbit may tilt its head toward the affected side and scratch persistently. Both ears are often affected.

Do not attempt to remove the crusts manually. They are attached to inflamed skin and pulling them tears the tissue below, causing bleeding and secondary bacterial infection. Leave crust removal to the treatment process; antiparasitic drops soften the crust and kill the mites, allowing the material to loosen and fall away naturally over 10 to 14 days.

Ear mite treatment in rabbits requires a prescription antiparasitic from a vet. Ivermectin-based or selamectin-based treatments are the standard approach and are applied as a spot-on. Do not use treatments licensed for cats or dogs in rabbits without veterinary advice; some permethrin-containing products are toxic to rabbits. Contact your vet for the correct product and dose.

Mites spread by direct contact. Isolate the affected rabbit while under treatment. Strip and thoroughly clean the hutch or run; mites can survive off the host for a limited time on bedding and surfaces. Treat all in-contact rabbits, even those showing no signs. Bedding should be removed and replaced completely on the day treatment begins.

After treatment the crust will gradually loosen and fall away over 1 to 2 weeks as the ear canal heals. Head shaking and scratching should reduce within a few days of starting treatment. If signs persist or the rabbit develops a head tilt that remains after the visible crust has cleared, the infection may have extended to the middle ear; this requires a vet assessment.
